graph.confusable_reason: Both are soilborne pathogens of squash that produce crown/collar rot, vascular involvement, wilting, and vine collapse, so in the field a plant that wilts and dies from a rotted base can be attributed to either. The overlap is greatest when only the wilted, collapsing plant with a brown crown is examined without looking for surface signs or assessing speed.
graph.shared_presentation: A squash plant that wilts and collapses associated with rot at the soil line. Both cause crown/collar/root rot, water-soaked-to-brown basal tissue, vascular discoloration, and eventual death of the vine, and both originate from soilborne inoculum.

forks.question: How fast does the plant go from healthy to collapsed?
forks.a_signal: Explosive, sudden wilt and collapse within days, often following heavy rain or flooding; plants down in patches almost overnight
forks.b_signal: Slow, chronic decline with stunting and gradual vine deterioration, typically late-season yield decline rather than rapid death

forks.question: What were the moisture and weather conditions preceding symptoms?
forks.a_signal: Warm, saturated soil, standing water, low spots in field, heavy rainfall or irrigation just before outbreak
forks.b_signal: Stress-associated chronic decline not tied to a flooding event; often cooler-season or accumulated stress, soilborne complex

forks.question: Are the fruit affected, especially fruit in contact with soil?
forks.a_signal: Fruit develop large soft-rot lesions, especially on the soil-contact side, that rapidly rot the whole fruit
forks.b_signal: Disease centers on roots, crown, and vascular tissue; fruit not characteristically rotted with surface mold

forks.question: What is the texture and character of the basal/crown rot?
forks.a_signal: Soft, watery, water-soaked rot of crown and stem that collapses quickly
forks.b_signal: Firmer rot/discoloration of crown and roots with vascular browning and stunting; dry decline rather than soft watery collapse

forks.question: What sign appears on the rotted crown or fruit surface under humid conditions (examine the surface)?
forks.a_signal: Abundant dense white, powdery-sugar-like sporulation (sporangia of an oomycete) coats infected crown and fruit
forks.b_signal: No white powdery oomycete bloom; at most sparse pale/cream fungal growth, with diagnosis requiring culture/microscopy of acervuli and slimy conidial masses

graph.narrative_a: Phytophthora Blight (Phytophthora capsici) is an aggressive oomycete that strikes after warm, saturated-soil conditions. Plants in low or flooded areas wilt and collapse rapidly, often in expanding patches following rain. The crown and stem show soft, water-soaked rot, and fruit touching the soil develop large soft-rot lesions. The hallmark sign is abundant dense white, powdered-sugar-like sporulation (sporangia) on rotted crowns and fruit under humid conditions.
graph.narrative_b: Plectosphaerella Fungus (Plectosphaerella cucumerina) is a soilborne ascomycete associated with chronic root, crown, and collar rot and late-season vine decline in squash. It causes stunting, vascular discoloration, and gradual collapse rather than explosive death, and it is frequently part of a soilborne fungal complex contributing to yield decline. It lacks the conspicuous white oomycete bloom; confirmation typically requires culturing and microscopic identification of its acervuli and slimy conidial masses.
graph.management_a: Improve drainage and avoid planting in low/wet spots; use raised beds, rotate out of cucurbits/solanaceous hosts for several years, avoid moving soil/water between fields, and apply oomycete-specific fungicides (e.g., mefenoxam/oxathiapiprolin) preventively. Do not harvest or leave infected fruit that sporulate.
graph.management_b: Manage as a soilborne decline complex: rotate with non-host crops, reduce plant stress, improve soil health and drainage, use clean transplants and sanitation, and remove crop debris. Confirm by lab culture/microscopy since it co-occurs with Fusarium and other soil fungi; broad-spectrum soilborne fungicide programs and resistant/tolerant cultivars where available help.

lay.farmer_lay_report: My squash plant suddenly wilted and flopped over, and down at the soil line the stem went soft and rotted, turning water-soaked and then brown. The vine is dying back and the rot seems to have started right at the base near the ground.
lay.decisive_lay_a: Look for a heavy, bright-white powdery or sugar-like fuzzy coating blanketing the rotted crown and any infected fruit, especially in humid or wet weather.
lay.decisive_lay_b: No white powdery bloom at all; at most a thin, sparse pale or cream-colored growth. You really can't tell it apart by eye and would need a lab to look at the tiny spore structures and slimy spore goo under a microscope.
